does: This paper introduces OmnixR, a comprehensive evaluation suite for benchmarking omni-modality language models like GPT-4o and Gemini that integrate text, vision, and audio. The suite consists of two components: a synthetic subset generated through Omnify, which systematically translates text-based tasks into multiple modalities including audio, images, video, and hybrid combinations; and a realistic subset of manually curated and expert-annotated real-world scenarios. OmnixR evaluates models on their ability to perform holistic reasoning and understanding that spans across modalities simultaneously, rather than processing modalities in isolation. The evaluation assesses performance on diverse task combinations such as questions involving simultaneous video, audio, and text inputs, providing a rigorous methodology for measuring cross-modal reasoning capabilities that existing benchmarks fail to capture.
connects: This work extends the tradition of multimodal benchmarking research that has progressively moved from single-modality evaluation toward dual-modality tasks. It responds to limitations in existing evaluation frameworks like VQA, VideoQA, and audio-visual datasets that typically assess understanding within bounded modal combinations. OmnixR builds on the broader effort to create comprehensive evaluation suites for large language models while adapting these approaches to the emerging challenge of truly omni-modal models. The paper acknowledges that prior work has focused on restricted modal combinations, leaving gaps in understanding how models handle complex cross-modal reasoning. It connects to research on synthetic data generation for evaluation and expert-annotated benchmarks for capturing natural linguistic and perceptual phenomena.
enables: This evaluation framework enables more rigorous assessment of the next generation of foundation models and directs future development toward genuine cross-modal reasoning capabilities. By providing structured benchmarks for omni-modality understanding, OmnixR allows researchers to identify specific failure modes and bottlenecks in multi-modal reasoning that single- or dual-modal evaluations would miss. The work points toward developing better training methodologies and architectures that achieve true integration across modalities rather than sequential processing. It establishes a methodology that can be extended to additional modalities and more complex reasoning tasks, supporting the evaluation of increasingly capable omni-modal systems and enabling developers to optimize models for real-world scenarios where users naturally combine multiple information sources.
